rows.v: Hi / Bye
CHAH-oh
Ciao, come stai? — Hi, how are you?
Informal, for both hello and goodbye. The 'c' before 'i' is a 'ch' sound.
World-famous and casual. Use only with friends and peers; for formality use 'salve' or 'buongiorno'. Originally from Venetian 'your slave (servant)'.
Good morning / Good day
bwohn-JOR-noh
Buongiorno, signora. — Good morning, ma'am.
'Buon' (good) + 'giorno' (day). The 'gi' is a soft 'j' sound.
The standard polite daytime greeting. Switches to 'buonasera' in the late afternoon.
Good evening
bwoh-nah-SEH-rah
Buonasera a tutti. — Good evening, everyone.
'Buona' (good, fem.) + 'sera' (evening). Used as both greeting and farewell in the evening.
Begin using it from the late afternoon onward. For bedtime, switch to 'buonanotte'.
Good night
bwoh-nah-NOT-teh
Buonanotte, a domani. — Good night, see you tomorrow.
'Buona' + 'notte' (night). Double 't' is held slightly longer — a feature of Italian.
Only for going to sleep, like English 'good night'. Don't use it as an evening greeting.
Goodbye
ah-ree-veh-DEHR-chee
Arrivederci, grazie. — Goodbye, thank you.
Literally 'to seeing each other again'. The polite, neutral goodbye. Formal version: 'arrivederLa'.
More polite than 'ciao' for goodbye. The rolled 'r' and the 'ci' (chee) sound are good pronunciation practice.
See you soon
ah PREH-stoh
A presto! — See you soon!
'A' (to/until) + 'presto' (soon/early). A warm farewell.
Use when you'll see someone again shortly. 'A dopo' (see you later) is another common option.
How are you? (informal)
KOH-meh STAH-ee
Come stai? — Bene! — How are you? — Good!
Uses 'stare' (to be/stay) for wellbeing, informal 'tu'. Formal version: 'Come sta?'.
Italian uses 'stare' (not 'essere') to ask how someone is. The formal 'Come sta?' is for strangers and elders.
Well / Good
BEH-neh
Sto bene, grazie. — I'm well, thanks.
An adverb — invariable. 'Molto bene' = very well; 'benissimo' = great.
The standard reply to 'come stai?'. Stack it up: 'bene' → 'molto bene' → 'benissimo'.
Nice to meet you
pyah-CHEH-reh
Piacere, sono Marco. — Nice to meet you, I'm Marco.
Literally 'pleasure'. Also the verb 'to please/like'. As a greeting it's a fixed phrase.
Said when meeting someone. A common fuller form is 'piacere di conoscerti' (pleasure to meet you).
What's your name? (informal)
KOH-meh tee KYAH-mee
Come ti chiami? — Mi chiamo Anna. — What's your name? — I'm Anna.
Reflexive 'chiamarsi' (to call oneself), informal. The 'chi' is a hard 'k' sound: 'kyah-mee'.
Answer 'Mi chiamo…' (I'm called…). Formal version: 'Come si chiama?'.
Yes
SEE
Sì, grazie. — Yes, thank you.
Accent matters: 'sì' (yes) vs 'si' (the reflexive 'oneself'). Pronounced 'see'.
The accent on 'sì' distinguishes 'yes' from the unstressed 'si'. A small mark, a big difference.
No
NOH
No, grazie. — No, thank you.
Invariable. To negate a verb, place 'non' before it ('non parlo' = I don't speak).
Note: 'no' is the answer 'no', but 'non' is the word that negates verbs. Don't mix them up.
Please
pehr fah-VOH-reh
Un caffè, per favore. — A coffee, please.
Literally 'for favor'. A fixed phrase; 'per piacere' and 'per cortesia' are alternatives.
The standard 'please'. Italians have several polite variants; 'per favore' is the most common and safe.
Thank you
GRAH-tsyeh
Grazie mille. — Thanks a lot.
Invariable. The 'z' is a 'ts' sound. 'Grazie mille' (a thousand thanks) intensifies it.
The 'zie' ending sounds like 'tsyeh'. 'Grazie mille' is the everyday way to say 'thanks so much'.
You're welcome
PREH-goh
— Grazie. — Prego. — Thanks. — You're welcome.
From 'pregare' (to pray/beg). A versatile word: 'you're welcome', 'please go ahead', 'after you'.
Wonderfully multipurpose: it answers 'grazie', invites someone to go first, or means 'please, help yourself'.
Excuse me (formal)
SKOO-zee
Scusi, dov'è la stazione? — Excuse me, where's the station?
Formal command from 'scusare'. Informal is 'scusa'.
Use 'scusi' (formal) with strangers, 'scusa' (informal) with friends, to apologize or get attention.
I'm sorry
mee dee-SPYAH-cheh
Mi dispiace molto. — I'm very sorry.
Literally 'it displeases me' — the thing is the subject, like 'mi piace'. For genuine regret.
Deeper than 'scusa': use 'mi dispiace' for real apology or sympathy, 'scusa' for minor bumps.
I don't understand
nohn kah-PEE-skoh
Scusi, non capisco. — Sorry, I don't understand.
From 'capire' (to understand), an '-isc-' verb. 'Non' negates it.
A survival phrase. Follow with 'può ripetere?' (can you repeat?) to keep things moving.
Do you speak English?
PAR-lah een-GLEH-zeh
Parla inglese? — Do you speak English?
Formal 'Lei' form of 'parlare'. Informal: 'Parli inglese?'. 'Inglese' is lowercase.
Italian doesn't capitalize languages. The formal 'parla' vs informal 'parli' is a one-letter politeness shift.
One
OO-noh
Uno, due, tre. — One, two, three.
Becomes 'un'/'uno' (masc.) or 'una'/'un'' (fem.) as the article 'a/an', depending on the next sound.
Like 'a/an', 'uno' shifts form to match the noun: 'un caffè', 'una pizza', 'un'amica'.
Two
DOO-eh
Due caffè, per favore. — Two coffees, please.
Invariable. Two clear vowels: 'DOO-eh'.
From two on, Italian numbers don't change for gender. Every vowel is pronounced distinctly.
Three
TREH
Sono le tre. — It's three o'clock.
Invariable. Just one syllable: 'treh'.
Used for counting and telling time: 'sono le tre' = it's three o'clock.
Ten
DYEH-chee
Ho dieci euro. — I have ten euros.
Invariable. The 'ci' is a 'chee' sound: 'DYEH-chee'.
Note 'euro' stays singular in Italian even when counting: 'dieci euro', not 'euros'.
One hundred
CHEHN-toh
Cento euro. — A hundred euros.
Invariable, even in multiples ('duecento' = 200, written as one word).
Unlike Spanish or French, 'cento' never changes and joins to its multiplier: 'duecento', 'trecento'.
What time is it?
keh OH-rah EH
Che ora è? — Sono le due. — What time is it? — It's two.
Literally 'what hour is it?'. Answer 'è l'una' (1:00) or 'sono le…' (other hours).
Singular 'è l'una' for one o'clock, plural 'sono le…' for the rest — the same pattern as Spanish.
Today
OH-jee
Oggi è lunedì. — Today is Monday.
Adverb of time. Double 'g' before 'i' gives a soft 'j': 'OH-jee'.
Pairs with 'ieri' (yesterday) and 'domani' (tomorrow) — the essential time trio.
Tomorrow
doh-MAH-nee
A domani! — See you tomorrow!
Adverb of time. 'A domani' is a common goodbye. 'Dopodomani' = the day after tomorrow.
Unlike Spanish 'mañana', Italian 'domani' means only 'tomorrow'; morning is 'mattina'.
Now
ah-DEHS-soh
Andiamo adesso. — Let's go now.
Adverb. 'Ora' is a common synonym ('now', also 'hour').
Both 'adesso' and 'ora' mean 'now'. 'Subito' means 'immediately / right away'.
Water
AH-kwah
Un bicchiere d'acqua, per favore. — A glass of water, please.
Feminine: 'l'acqua'. The 'cqu' is a held 'kw' sound, unique to a few Italian words.
Ask for 'acqua naturale' (still) or 'acqua frizzante' (sparkling) — Italians will often ask which you want.
Coffee
kahf-FEH
Un caffè, per favore. — A coffee, please.
Masculine: 'il caffè'. The accent on the final 'è' means the stress lands there: 'kahf-FEH'.
Ordering 'un caffè' gets you an espresso. A 'caffè latte' is coffee with milk — but just 'latte' will get you a glass of milk!
Bread
PAH-neh
Del pane, per favore. — Some bread, please.
Masculine: 'il pane'. Two clear syllables: 'PAH-neh'.
A staple at every Italian meal, usually served without butter. A bakery is 'il forno' or 'la panetteria'.
The bill / check
eel KOHN-toh
Il conto, per favore. — The check, please.
Masculine: 'il conto'. From 'contare' (to count).
As elsewhere in Europe, the bill comes only when you ask. Catch the waiter with 'il conto, per favore'.
Wine
VEE-noh
Un bicchiere di vino rosso. — A glass of red wine.
Masculine: 'il vino'. 'Vino rosso' (red), 'vino bianco' (white).
Central to Italian dining. 'Un quartino' is a quarter-litre carafe — a handy, common house-wine order.
Food
CHEE-boh
Il cibo è ottimo. — The food is excellent.
Masculine: 'il cibo'. The 'ci' is 'chee': 'CHEE-boh'. A general word for food.
'Il cibo' is food in general. For a specific dish you'd say 'il piatto'; a meal is 'il pasto'.
I would like
vohr-RAY
Vorrei un caffè. — I'd like a coffee.
Polite conditional of 'volere' (to want). Softer than 'voglio' (I want).
The courteous way to order. 'Vorrei…' (I'd like) is gentler than the blunt 'voglio…' (I want).
The menu
eel meh-NOO
Il menù, per favore. — The menu, please.
Masculine: 'il menù', with the stress on the final 'u'. A loanword from French.
Unlike French/Spanish, in Italy 'il menù' simply means the menu of dishes. 'Il menù fisso' is a set meal.
Cheers! / Bless you!
sah-LOO-teh
Salute! — Cheers!
Literally 'health'. Used as a toast and after a sneeze. 'Cin cin' is the casual clink-glasses toast.
Toast with 'salute' or the playful 'cin cin'. Like Spanish 'salud', it also means 'bless you' after a sneeze.
Where is…?
doh-VEH
Dov'è la stazione? — Where is the station?
'Dove' (where) + 'è' (is) contracts to 'dov'è'. Plural: 'dove sono…?'.
The essential travel question. 'Dove' elides with 'è' to 'dov'è' — a smooth, common contraction.
Bathroom
BAH-nyoh
Dov'è il bagno? — Where's the bathroom?
Masculine: 'il bagno'. The 'gn' is a 'ny' sound, like Spanish 'ñ'.
'Il bagno' covers both bathroom and bath. In a bar or restaurant you can also ask for 'la toilette'.
Left
see-NEE-strah
Giri a sinistra. — Turn left.
Feminine, used in 'a sinistra' (to the left).
Direction basics: 'a sinistra' (left), 'a destra' (right), 'dritto' / 'sempre dritto' (straight ahead).
Right
DEH-strah
È a destra. — It's on the right.
Feminine: 'la destra'. Don't confuse with 'dritto' (straight ahead).
Trap to watch: 'a destra' = to the right, but 'dritto' = straight ahead. Listen carefully when getting directions.
Here
KWEE
Sono qui. — I'm here.
Adverb of place. 'Qui' and 'qua' both mean 'here', used almost interchangeably.
Italian has two near-identical 'here' words (qui/qua) and two for 'there' (lì/là). All are common.
There
LAH
È là. — It's over there.
Adverb of place, with accent: 'là' (there) vs 'la' (the). 'Lì' is a synonym.
The accent distinguishes 'là' (there) from 'la' (the feminine article). 'Lì' and 'là' both mean 'there'.
Station
stah-TSYOH-neh
La stazione è vicina. — The station is nearby.
Feminine: 'la stazione'. Words ending in '-zione' are feminine (like Spanish '-ción').
A reliable rule: '-zione' nouns are feminine ('la stazione', 'la lezione'). The 'zi' sounds like 'tsy'.
How much does it cost?
KWAHN-toh KOH-stah
Quanto costa? — How much is it?
From 'costare' (to cost). For multiple items: 'quanto costano?'.
The key shopping question. 'Quant'è?' (how much is it?) is a shorter, common alternative.
I
EE-oh
Io sono italiano. — I am Italian.
Subject pronoun. Often dropped, since the verb ending shows the subject ('sono' = I am).
Like Spanish, Italian usually omits subject pronouns — 'sono' alone means 'I am'. Use 'io' for emphasis.
You (informal)
TOO
Tu parli italiano? — Do you speak Italian?
Informal singular 'you'. The formal equivalent is 'Lei' (capitalized).
Use 'tu' with friends and peers; switch to the formal 'Lei' for strangers, elders, and business.
He / She
LOO-ee / LEH-ee
Lui è alto. Lei è alta. — He is tall. She is tall.
'Lui' (he), 'lei' (she). Capitalized 'Lei' is also the formal 'you'. Adjectives agree: 'alto'/'alta'.
'Lei' does double duty: lowercase 'lei' = she, capitalized 'Lei' = formal 'you'. Adjective endings shift by gender.
We
NOH-ee
Noi siamo amici. — We are friends.
Subject pronoun 'we'. Two vowels: 'NOH-ee'.
The verb ending ('siamo' = we are) already signals 'we', so 'noi' is often dropped except for emphasis.
Friend
ah-MEE-koh
È il mio migliore amico. — He's my best friend.
'l'amico' (male), 'l'amica' (female). Plurals: 'amici' (the 'c' softens to 'chee') / 'amiche' (stays hard 'k').
A spelling quirk: masculine plural 'amici' is 'ah-MEE-chee', but feminine 'amiche' keeps the hard 'k' sound.
Family
fah-MEE-lyah
La mia famiglia è grande. — My family is big.
Feminine: 'la famiglia'. The 'gli' is a liquid 'ly' sound, distinctive to Italian.
The 'gli' sound (as in 'famiglia', 'figlio') has no English equivalent — somewhere between 'ly' and 'lli'.
Mother
MAH-dreh
Mia madre si chiama Sofia. — My mother's name is Sofia.
Feminine: 'la madre'. Affectionate: 'mamma'.
Note: with singular family members you drop the article — 'mia madre', not 'la mia madre'. 'Mamma' is the warm term.
Father
PAH-dreh
Mio padre lavora molto. — My father works a lot.
Masculine: 'il padre'. 'I genitori' means 'the parents'. Affectionate: 'papà'.
Italian uses a separate word, 'i genitori', for 'parents' (not the plural of padre). Affectionate: 'papà' (stress on the end).
Man / Woman
WOH-moh / DOHN-nah
L'uomo e la donna. — The man and the woman.
'l'uomo' (man, irregular plural 'uomini'), 'la donna' (woman, plural 'donne').
'Uomo' has an irregular plural: one 'uomo', many 'uomini'. 'Donna' is regular: 'donne'.
To be
EHS-seh-reh
Sono stanco. — I am tired.
Highly irregular: sono, sei, è, siamo, siete, sono. Used for identity, origin, traits.
Italian, like Spanish, has two 'to be' verbs. 'Essere' is for permanent identity and is the main past-tense helper.
To have
ah-VEH-reh
Ho due fratelli. — I have two brothers.
Irregular: ho, hai, ha, abbiamo, avete, hanno. The 'h' is always silent.
Used for age and hunger like Spanish: 'ho fame' (I'm hungry), 'ho 20 anni' (I'm 20). The silent 'h' distinguishes 'ho' from 'o' (or).
To be / stay
STAH-reh
Come stai? — Sto bene. — How are you? — I'm well.
Irregular: sto, stai, sta, stiamo, state, stanno. Used for health, location, and ongoing actions.
Italian uses 'stare' (not 'essere') for how you feel and for the '-ing' present: 'sto mangiando' = I'm eating.
To go
ahn-DAH-reh
Vado a casa. — I'm going home.
Irregular: vado, vai, va, andiamo, andate, vanno.
'Andiamo!' (let's go!) is one of the most useful exclamations you'll hear and use in Italy.
To speak
par-LAH-reh
Parlo un po' di italiano. — I speak a little Italian.
Regular '-are' verb — the model for the largest group. parlo, parli, parla, parliamo, parlate, parlano.
Learn 'parlare' and you have the pattern for thousands of '-are' verbs, Italian's biggest verb family.
To eat
mahn-JAH-reh
Vuoi mangiare qualcosa? — Do you want to eat something?
'-are' verb with a spelling tweak: the 'i' disappears in 'mangi', 'mangiamo' to keep the soft 'g'.
A near-regular '-are' verb. The silent 'i' in 'mangiamo' just preserves the soft 'g' (j) sound.
To want
voh-LEH-reh
Voglio un caffè. — I want a coffee.
Irregular: voglio, vuoi, vuole, vogliamo, volete, vogliono. The 'gli' is that liquid 'ly' sound.
'Voglio' (I want) is direct; 'vorrei' (I'd like) is the polite form for ordering and requests.
Can / to be able to
poh-TEH-reh
Puoi aiutarmi? — Can you help me?
Irregular: posso, puoi, può, possiamo, potete, possono. Followed by an infinitive.
Essential for requests: 'può…?' (formal) / 'puoi…?' (informal) = 'can you…?'. 'Posso?' = 'may I?'.
To do / make
FAH-reh
Che cosa fai? — What are you doing?
Irregular: faccio, fai, fa, facciamo, fate, fanno. Covers both 'do' and 'make'.
Also drives weather and many idioms: 'fa caldo' (it's hot), 'fare una foto' (to take a photo). A workhorse verb.
No problem
nehs-SOON proh-BLEH-mah
— Grazie. — Nessun problema. — Thanks. — No problem.
'Nessun' (no/none) + 'problema'. Note 'problema' is masculine despite ending in '-a'.
A relaxed reply to thanks. Watch out: 'il problema' is masculine — a common exception to the '-a = feminine' rule.
OK / Agreed
dahk-KOR-doh
— Andiamo? — D'accordo. — Shall we go? — OK.
From 'essere d'accordo' (to agree). 'D'accordo' alone = OK / agreed.
The everyday 'OK'. 'Sono d'accordo' = 'I agree'. Very common in conversation.
Of course / Sure
CHEHR-toh
— Vieni? — Certo! — Are you coming? — Of course!
An adverb/adjective. 'Certo!' on its own = of course! 'Certamente' is the fuller form.
An enthusiastic 'of course!'. The 'ce' sounds like 'cheh': 'CHEHR-toh'.
I'm hungry
oh FAH-meh
Ho fame, mangiamo? — I'm hungry, shall we eat?
Uses 'avere' (to have) + 'fame' (hunger): literally 'I have hunger'. The 'h' is silent.
Like Spanish and French, Italian 'has' hunger. Similarly 'ho sete' = I'm thirsty.
I'm lost
mee SOH-noh PEHR-soh
Scusi, mi sono perso. — Sorry, I'm lost.
Reflexive past of 'perdersi' (to get lost). A woman says 'persa'.
The ending agrees with gender: a man says 'perso', a woman 'persa'. A vital travel phrase.
I like (it)
mee PYAH-cheh
Mi piace il caffè. — I like coffee.
From 'piacere' — works backwards: literally 'it is pleasing to me'. Plural things: 'mi piacciono'.
Like Spanish 'me gusta', the thing liked is the subject: 'mi piacciono i gatti' = 'cats are pleasing to me' = I like cats.
Enjoy your meal
bwohn ahp-peh-TEE-toh
Buon appetito! — Enjoy your meal!
Literally 'good appetite'. A fixed phrase, said before eating.
Always said before a meal in Italy. The polite reply, if someone says it to you, is 'grazie, altrettanto' (thanks, you too).
How much is it?
kwahn-TEH
Quant'è? — How much is it?
'Quanto' (how much) + 'è' (is), contracted to 'quant'è'. The casual price question.
'Quant'è?' is the quick spoken form; 'quanto costa?' is slightly more complete. Both are everywhere.
